title: Chicken piccata
subtitle: Pan-fried chicken cutlets with a lemon and caper butter sauce
excerpt: Thinly pounded chicken breast cutlets coated in flour and fried in butter and oil until golden, then a quick pan sauce made from white wine, chicken stock, lemon juice, and capers. A weeknight dish that cooks in 20 minutes once the chicken is flattened.
sourceNotes: Chicken piccata is an Italian-American dish developed in the United States, adapted from the Italian scaloppine method applied to veal. The lemon and caper sauce follows the piccata pattern documented in mid-twentieth-century Italian-American cookery.

content.text: The chicken must be thin (about 5 mm at the thickest point) so it cooks through quickly before the coating burns. Either buy thin-cut chicken breasts or butterfly and pound standard ones between sheets of cling film. The flour coating gives the sauce something to cling to; the same flour thickens the pan juices slightly as the butter melts into them.

Work in batches so the pan stays hot. A crowded pan drops the temperature and the chicken stews rather than fries. Keep the cooked pieces warm in a low oven while you finish the second batch, then make the sauce in the same pan with the fond left over from the frying.

Place each {{chicken-breast}} chicken breast between two sheets of cling film and pound with a meat mallet until about 5 mm thick. Season lightly with salt and pepper. Dredge each piece in the {{plain-flour}} of seasoned flour, shaking off any excess.

Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and half the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large frying pan over medium-high heat until the butter foams. Fry the cutlets two at a time for 2 to 3 minutes per side until golden brown and cooked through. Transfer to a warm plate and repeat with the remaining cutlets.

Pour the {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine into the same pan and cook over high heat, scraping up any stuck bits, until reduced by half. Add the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and the lemon juice from the {{lemon}} lemons. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes until the liquid has reduced by a third. Add the {{capers}} of capers. Remove from the heat and whisk in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} of butter cubes a piece at a time until the sauce is glossy. Stir in the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley. Taste and adjust the seasoning.
Return the chicken to the pan for 30 seconds to warm through in the sauce. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Pound to 5 mm and cook for no more than 3 minutes per side; a thin cutlet needs very little time
items.cause: Cutlets not thin enough, or left in the pan too long

items.fix: Reduce the wine and stock until slightly syrupy before adding the butter; add the butter off the heat or over very low heat and whisk as it melts
items.cause: Not reduced enough, or cold butter added over heat

items.fix: Wait until the cutlet releases naturally, if it sticks when you try to flip it, it is not ready; the crust needs 30 more seconds
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or moved the chicken too soon
items.symptom: Coating has stuck to the pan rather than the chicken

content.text: Piccata means sharp or pointed in Italian, referring to the sharpness of the lemon and caper sauce. The method originates with veal scaloppine, thin veal cutlets cooked in the same way. The chicken version became the standard in Italian-American cooking during the mid-twentieth century, when chicken was more accessible and less expensive than veal in the United States. It has since crossed back into Italian cooking, where it sits alongside other pan-sauce preparations as a straightforward weeknight dish.
